coredata.dc:title: Room-temperature phosphorescent fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots: Information encryption and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Feng
Li, Zeyu
Li, Yu
Feng, Yiyu
Feng, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions is a limitation for achieving a carbon dots-based material with room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP). Here, fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs), prepared using the solvothermal method and further gas-phase fluorination from fructose and diethylenetriamine (DETA), were found to exhibit RTP lifetime and quantum yield of 1.14 s and 8.3%. By comparing the structure and performance of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and FNCDs, it was found that the RTP of FNCDs originates from the π→π∗ and n→π∗ electron transitions C–N/CN, which can be attributed to the small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states. We further explored the mechanism of RTP by analyzing the hydrogen bonding between carbon dots and polyvinyl alcohol matrix. The semi-ionic C–F bonds also enhance intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonding and reduce the quenching of RTP without the oxygen barrier. Furthermore, we applied the prepared aqueous FNCDs as an advanced security ink for information printing and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescence
Fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
Information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the procedures for the preparation of NCDs and FNCDs. (b) TEM image and (c) High-resolution TEM image of FNCDs. (d) Particle size distribution of FNCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) FT-IR and (b) XPS spectra of the NCDs and FNCDs. High-resolution XPS fitting results for (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s, and (f) F1s spectra of NCDs and FNCDs, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The normalized UV–vis absorbance (black curve), fluorescence emission (right blue curve), and excitation (left blue curve) spectra of the (a) NCDs and (b) FNCDs in water. (c) Normalized fluorescence (blue curve) and phosphorescence (black and red curves) emission and excitation spectra of the FNCDs powder. (d) 3D phosphorescence plot of the FNCDs powder. (e) RTP decay spectra of the FNCDs powder, recorded at emission wavelengths of 500 nm under excitation at 328 nm. (f) Illustration of the possible triple-mode emission process of FNCDs powder. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a, b) Printed images using (a) RTP FNCDs-based and (b) interference NCDs-based inks on commercial paper under a 365 nm UV lamp for on and off situation. (c) Images of anti-counterfeiting and information protection applications of the FNCDs (QR code under daylight, 365 nm UV lamp for on and off situations). (d). Digital images of school badge on filter paper printed using FNCDs ink, and irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp for on and off situations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Room-temperature phosphorescent fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots: Information encryption and anti-counterfeiting

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Composite and Functional Materials, Tianjin, 300072, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Composite and Functional Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions is a limitation for achieving a carbon dots-based material with room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP). Here, fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs), prepared using the solvothermal method and further gas-phase fluorination from fructose and diethylenetriamine (DETA), were found to exhibit RTP lifetime and quantum yield of 1.14 s and 8.3%. By comparing the structure and performance of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and FNCDs, it was found that the RTP of FNCDs originates from the π→π∗ and n→π∗ electron transitions C–N/CN, which can be attributed to the small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states. We further explored the mechanism of RTP by analyzing the hydrogen bonding between carbon dots and polyvinyl alcohol matrix. The semi-ionic C–F bonds also enhance intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonding and reduce the quenching of RTP without the oxygen barrier. Furthermore, we applied the prepared aqueous FNCDs as an advanced security ink for information printing and anti-counterfeiting.

The effective conversion of fluorescence to room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots is achieved by fluorinating NCDs to enhance intersystem crossing and stabilize the triplet-excited states. The NCD and FNCD aqueous solutions made into advanced anti-counterfeiting inks have the prospect of being used in information printing and anti-counterfeiting.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescent materials have attracted significant attention attributed to their promising applications in bioimaging, biosensing, optoelectronic devices, data encryption, etc. [1–10] However, regular commercial phosphorescent materials-semiconductor quantum dots composed of organometallic complexes and metal-free pure organic compounds have drawbacks such as cost ineffectiveness, high toxicity, complex preparation, and easy quenching by oxygen in the environment [3,4,7,9]. As an emerging light-emitting nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have become a better alternative replacing semiconductor quantum dots due to their low toxicity, cost-effective preparation methods, excellent biocompatibility, and photostability [11–16].

Generally, the formation of phosphorescent CDs is challenging, owing to the spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions. Several approaches for forming/fabricating phosphorescent CDs have been reported. Such strategies include embedding CDs in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyurethane (PU), polyacrylic acid (PAA), polyacrylamide (PAM), silica, and urea/biuret [17–24], which suppress the nonradiative vibration and stabilize triplet-excited states. However, the application of phosphorescent CDs is limited due to their insolubility and easy quenching by oxygen and at high temperatures. Thus, the surface passivation approach by introducing halogen or dopant elements had been previously studied. Compared with single-element-doped CDs (nitrogen-doped CDs), double-element-doped CDs (including nitrogen-sulfur co-doped or nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped CDs) show higher quantum yield and longer phosphorescence lifetime because the dopant heteroatoms can generate new energy levels and promote radiation binding [12,25–28].

Inspired by the abovementioned viewpoint, we propose that phosphorescent CDs can be obtained by fluorine-nitrogen co-doping, because fluorine-nitrogen co-doping can increase the number of triplet excitons and that multiple replacements of fluorine can increase the planarity of the molecule. Also, semi-ionic C–F bonds can form hydrogen bonds, reducing the quenching of phosphorescence by oxygen [29]. Several studies have reported the development of FNCDs by hydrothermal fluorination. This approach is mild and the fluorination degree of the prepared product is relatively low (<15%), resulting in low quantum yield and phosphorescence lifetime [30]. Gas-phase fluorination is another considered approach because the degree of fluorination of carbon materials can be adjusted by controlling the temperature [31–33].

Here, we report novel FNCDs prepared by a solvothermal method and further gas-phase fluorination, using fructose and diethylenetriamine as precursors (Fig. 1 a). Compared with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), the fluorescence quantum yield of FNCDs was improved from 25.8% to 54.17%, which agrees with that of a previous study on ethylenediamine-based CDs analogs [34]. FNCDs powder exhibits green RTP performance, which is recognizable by the naked eye for 9 s after the removal of UV lamp (Figure S1, supporting information). Moreover, both NCDs and FNCDs exhibited good water solubility and bright blue fluorescence (Figure S2, supporting information). The FNCDs-coated paper shows self-protective RTP stability without oxygen protection, which can be maintained at 87.3% under 328 nm irradiation for 1 h. Based on these features, NCDs and FNCDs aqueous solution can be used as advanced anti-counterfeiting inks for information printing and anti-counterfeiting. To our best understanding, this work is a novel approach to convert fluorescent materials to room-temperature phosphorescent materials by further gas-phase fluorination.

Fructose (99%), diethylenetriamine (DETA), and hydrofluoric acid (40%) were purchased from TCI, Japan. Ethanol, tryptophan and dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (China) Co., Ltd. Deionized (DI) water was used in the experiments. PVA (Mw = 72000) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Unless otherwise noted, all reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial sources and used directly without further purification.

The raw NCDs were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method. 1.0 g fructose and 2 mL DETA were added to 40 mL ethanol in a Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After the solvothermal reaction, the solvent and DETA were removed by vacuum rotary evaporation at 50 °C to obtain a dark black dispersion, and the resulting black viscous solid was dissolved in water and added to a 300 D PTFE dialysis bag. Finally, after 24 h, the brown-black-colored powder NCDs were obtained by freeze-drying.

FNCDs were obtained by the gas-phase fluorination of NCDs. The synthesized NCDs powder was put into a Ni reactor and fluorinated with F2/N2 gas (3% ratio) and heating to 25 °C–125 °C (reaction temperature range) for 2 h to ensure complete reaction. Finally, the FNCDs were obtained as a gray powder.

0.5 mL CDs solution (5.0 mg mL−1 in water) was mixed with 1.5 mL PVA (Mw = 72000) solution (1.0 g PVA in 15 mL water). The obtained CDs-PVA solution was dropped into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) mold and dried naturally to obtain the PVA film.

FNCDs ink was obtained by dissolving FNCDs powder in deionized water by ultrasonic, which can be directly used as security printing ink for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. The interference NCDs ink was obtained in the same way.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on a high-resolution JEOL 2010F TEM/STEM. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were conducted with PERKIN ELMZR PHI 3056. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. Fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions and excitation spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer at ambient conditions. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 35 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes were measured using the FLS980 spectrometer. Photographs and videos of fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions were taken using a smartphone under excitation by a hand-held UV lamp (365 nm). Inks were printed using an HP Deskjet 1112.

To determine the origin of the luminescence behavior, the morphologies and structures of the NCDs and FNCDs were analyzed. The morphologies of NCDs and FNCDs were characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1b and S3 (supporting information). Both the NCDs and FNCDs were well-dispersed and exhibited a spherical shape with an amorphous feature, which agrees with that in a previous study on NCDs [29].

Both the NCDs and FNCDs high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1c and S4 (supporting information)) showed identical lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which is similar to the <100> facet of graphitic. The average size of NCDs is 4.7 nm, with a narrow size distribution from 3 to 6 nm (Figure S5, supporting information), while the average size of FNCDs increased to 4.8 nm, with the size distribution in the range of 3–8 nm (Fig. 1d). These results indicate that the gas-phase fluorination process does not destroy the lattice structure of NCDs but may affect only the surface structure.

To explore the gas-phase fluorination effect on the NCDs surface structure, FT-IR and XPS were conducted. As shown in the black spectrum in Fig. 2 a, the NCDs shows a broad absorption band from 3600 to 2500 cm−1. The peaks located at 3335, 3126, and 3021 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH, –NH2, and –CH2 groups. The absorption peaks at 1620 and 1516 cm−1 correspond to the bending and stretching vibrations of N–H and CN, respectively. Finally, the peaks located at 1402 and 1131 cm−1 could be caused by the stretching vibrations of C–O/C–N. Comparing the FT-IR spectra of the NCDs and FNCDs (the red spectrum in Fig. 2a) shows several differences, including the intensity-decreased peak range from 3600 to 3000 cm−1 and the intensity-increased peak at 1050 cm−1. The former may imply a decrease of the hydrophilic groups (-OH and –COOH) and the latter may be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–F because of gas-phase fluorination. Moreover, the peak intensities at 2978, 2914, and 1244 cm−1 increased, indicating the production of C–N. To verify the above FT-IR results, XPS characterization was further conducted. As shown in Fig. 2b and Table S1, the NCDs contain C, N, and O elements with compositions of 65.74%, 19.55%, and 14.71%, respectively, while the FNCDs mainly contain C, N, O, and F elements with compositions of 42.95%, 7.36%, 11.59%, and 38.10%.

The compositional analysis of the XPS spectrum shows an emergent F but decreased O and N contents, indicating the presence of F dopant elements and hydrophilic group loss from NCDs to FNCDs. These results are consistent with the FT-IR analysis. Furthermore, both the C1s spectra of the NCDs and FNCDs (Fig. 2c) imply the presence of C–C (284.9 eV), C–O/C–N (286.0 eV), and CO (288.8 eV) bonds, but with obvious emergent C–F (287.4 eV) and CF2 (290.6 eV) bonds resulting from NCDs to FNCDs. The high-resolution N1s spectra of NCDs and FNCDs (Fig. 2d) simultaneously show two main peaks of CN (398.8 eV) and amino N (400.1 eV). It has been previously reported that C–N/CN groups promote RTP and amide/amino N-type atoms facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds, respectively [35]. The high-resolution O1s spectra of NCDs and FNCDs (Fig. 2e) show two main peaks of C–O (531.8 eV) and CO (533 eV). Compared with NCDs, the CO/C–O ratio of FNCDs decreased, which is attributed to the heating process. The high-resolution F1s spectra of FNCDs (Fig. 2f) can be fitted with three main peaks of semi-ionic C–F (685.1 eV), covalent C–F (687.8 eV), and –CF2 (689.0 eV). The exceptional water solubility of FNCDs is attributed to the higher semi-ionic C–F and amide/amino type N, which was confirmed by the FT-IR and high-resolution F1s spectra [36].

Based on the HRTEM results, a FNCDs with an amorphous structure and graphite lattice fringes was determined. Thus, the FT-IR and XPS analyses confirmed that FNCDs contains abundant functional groups, such as –COOH, –OH, C–N/CN, and C–F. These functional groups may act as sub-fluorophores to promote the production of fluorescence and RTP or can form intermolecular or intramolecular hydrogen bonds to stabilize the triplet state. The high content of dopant elements (N and F) can also effectively promote intersystem crossing (ISC) to populate triplet excitons [30,35,36].

The Photoluminescence properties of NCDs and FNCDs were investigated. Fig. 3 a and b shows the normalized UV/Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the NCDs and FNCDs in aqueous solution, respectively. The UV/Vis absorption spectra of the NCDs and FNCDs exhibit broadband from 280 to 500 nm, which may be assigned to the n→π∗ and π→π∗ transitions of certain sub-fluorophores (e.g., C–N/CN, –NH2, and C–F). However, the UV absorption peak of the aqueous FNCDs bulges at 325 nm, which may be caused by the bathochromic shift of the π→π∗ transition when the F atom is substituted. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the aqueous NCDs show a maximum emission peak at 448 nm under 362 nm excitation, while that of the aqueous FNCDs shows a maximum emission peak at 450 nm under 370 nm excitation. The bathochromic shift of fluorescence excitation and emission spectra corresponds to the bathochromic shift of UV absorption. Notably, the NCDs and FNCDs aqueous solution simultaneously exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence emission (Figures S6 and S7, supporting information). Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence measurements were also performed on NCDs and FNCDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1). The results indicate that both CDs exhibited a single and narrow emission center in 3D fluorescence plots, which may be caused by narrow size distribution and homogeneous species (Figures S8 and Figure S9, supporting information). The absolute PL quantum yields of both CDs were also measured and found to increase from 25.80% NCDs to 51.94% FNCDs. The fluorescence intensity and quantum yield increase may be due to the planarity of the molecule caused by multi-substituted fluorine atoms and the formation of a hydrogen bond between the C–F bond and the substituent [29,35]. However, from Figure S2 (supporting information), no obvious difference exists in fluorescence intensity between both CDs, implying that NCDs aqueous solution can be used as an interference reagent of FNCDs ink.

The pH response of fluorescence emissions of NCDs and FNCDs aqueous solution were also measured. Figures S10 and S11 (supporting information) show that the maximum emission peaks of both CDs were maintained at different pH values. The maximum excitation peaks of NCDs remain stable as the pH changes in the range of 4–9, with only a slight reduction under strong acidity and alkalinity, While the maximum excitation peaks of FNCDs are stable above 85% in the whole pH range. This result may be because the aromatic sp2 domain with CC bond acts as the source of fluorescence emission and can maintain stability at different pH values. Moreover, the introduction of C–F can improve the planar rigidity of molecules, which makes FNCDs more stably-structured to avoid external environmental influence. As a result, NCDs and FNCDs aqueous solution can be used as safe inks over several pH values [29,36].

To understand the RTP properties of the FNCDs, the phosphorescence spectra of the FNCDs powder were measured. As shown in Fig. 3c, the optimal excitation wavelength is located at 328 nm, suggesting that phosphorescence mainly originates from n→π∗ electron transitions of CN. As shown in Fig. 3d, the 3D phosphorescence plot indicates that the FNCDs exhibit a broad emission center, with an optimal peak range of 400–500 nm at an excitation wavelength of 328 nm. FNCDs showed excitation-dependent phosphorescence features (Figure S12, supporting information), indicating the existence of multiple triplet-excited states. Also, the steady-state FL and afterglow decay spectra of the FNCDs were further measured. As expected, the FNCDs powder showed a 4.67 ns PL (Figure S13, supporting information) and ms-based phosphorescence (Fig. 3e) lifetimes. The afterglow decay spectrum of the FNCDs was fitted with a bi-exponential function with lifetime components of 117.6 ms (5.83%) and 1145.5 ms (94.17%), respectively. These results also imply that the FNCDs possess at least dual decay channels. FNCDs powder shows about 9 s green RTP, which is easily recognizable. According to the following equation:
τ avg
=
∑
α i
τ i 2
/ ∑
α i
τ i
average lifetime was calculated to be 1.14 s. As shown in the Table S2 (supporting information), phosphorescence lifetime and quantum yield (QY) of the reported works about phosphorescent nanoparticles are summarized. Compared with our previous work (FNCDs-coated paper), the phosphorescent QY had been improved from 3.45% to 8.3% [29]. To investigate the RTP performance of the FNCDs after being coated on a filter paper, we evaluated the self-protection RTP performance of FNCDs. The evaluation was conducted by coating water-based FNCDs (2.0 mg mL−1) on filter paper under ambient conditions without any oxygen barrier. The phosphorescence spectra of the FNCDs-coated paper and the FNCDs powder are shown in Figure S14 (supporting information). The phosphorescence patterns of both materials have the same maximum emission wavelength, and phosphorescence can be achieved without the aid of other substrates under aerobic conditions. This result may be attributed to the steric protection of semi-ionic C–F bonds on FNCDs surfaces. Moreover, the fluorine substitutes doped on FNCDs play the role of supporting inter- and intra-sites hydrogen bonds, thereby preventing oxygen from quenching phosphorescence at room temperature [29,31,35].

The relationship between the phosphorescence intensity and irradiation time of the FNCDs-coated paper was also experimentally investigated. As shown in Figure S15 (supporting information), the phosphorescence intensity of the FNCDs-coated paper slightly decreased to 87.3% after continuous excitation for 60 min, indicating excellent photostability of the FNCDs. The small energy gap (ΔEst) between the lowest singlet (S1) and triplet (T1) states indicates that electrons can produce effective ISC (Fig. 3f). As shown in Figure S16 (supporting information), the low temperature (77 K) fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of FNCDs display optimal emissions at 430 and 472 nm, respectively. Accordingly, the ΔEst was determined as 0.256 eV (S1 = 2.883 eV and T1 = 2.627 eV). Such a small value means an effective ISC process and easy generation of triplet excitons [17,37]. What's more, temperature-dependent phosphorescence emission spectra (Figure S17, supporting information) and temperature-dependent phosphorescence decay curves (Figure S18, supporting information) of FNCDs powder indicates that as the temperature rises from 77 K to 298 K, the maximum emission wavelength increases, the phosphorescence intensity gradually decreases, and the lifetime decreases from 1.90 s to 1.10 s.

To further explore the RTP origin of NCDs and FNCDs, corresponding films (NCDs-PVA and FNCDs-PVA films) were prepared by embedding NCDs and FNCDs in the PVA matrix. PVA is often combined with the CDs to facilitate phosphorescence formation [26], because the PVA matrix can form hydrogen bonds with the CDs to stabilize the triplet exciton and suppress the nonradiative vibration, thereby increasing the luminescence efficiency. As shown in Figure S19 (supporting information), NCDs-PVA and FNCDs-PVA films are colorless and transparent under daylight. Figures S20 and S21 (supporting information) show the fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of NCDs-PVA and FNCDs-PVA films. Under a 340 nm wavelength excitation, the maximum excitation wavelengths of the NCDs-PVA and FNCDs-PVA films are 406 and 439 nm, respectively. The bathochromic shifts of maximum excitation wavelength are consistent with those in aqueous solution, which may be caused by the bathochromic shift of the π→π∗ transition when the F atom is substituted. Besides, both the NCDs-PVA and FNCDs-PVA films exhibited RTP properties (Figures S22 and S23, supporting information). Furthermore, phosphorescence lifetime decay spectra of NCDs-PVA and FNCDs-PVA films were measured (Figure S24 and Table S3, supporting information). The phosphorescence lifetimes of the NCDs-PVA and FNCDs-PVA films were determined as 0.75 and 1.02 s, respectively. In the NCDs-PVA film, phosphorescence is attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the PVA and the carboxyl or amino groups on the surface of the NCDs. In the FNCDs-PVA film, in addition to the carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of the FNCDs, many semi-ionic C–F bonds exist, which can form intramolecular or intermolecular hydrogen bonds with PVA. Also, the introduction of F atoms can increase triplet excitons and the phosphorescence lifetime. In summary, the NCDs-PVA film has better RTP performance, while the FNCDs-PVA film has a long RTP life. These results not only verify that the generation of phosphorescence due to n→π∗ and π→π∗ electron transitions of C–N/CN can promote effective ISC, but also that the introduction of F atoms affects the phosphorescence lifetime enhancement.

To explore the influence of varying fluorination degrees on the phosphorescence performance of FNCDs, FNCDs with different fluorine contents were obtained by changing the gas-phase fluorination temperature. The results are shown in Figure S25 and Table S4 (supporting information). The fluorination degree and maximum emission wavelength gradually increase with the reaction temperature and remains unchanged at higher reaction temperatures. The maximum fluorination degree depends on the number of groups that can react with F2 on the surface of NCDs. The phosphorescence lifetime of different fluorine content is shown in Table S5. The FNCDs obtained at room temperature have a lower fluorine content and unobservable phosphorescence. After increasing the reaction temperature, the phosphorescence lifetime increased with the fluorine content. This result also verifies that the introduction of fluorine atoms enhances the phosphorescence lifetime of the FNCDs.

Based on the difference luminescence between NCDs and FNCDs-only green RTP is observed in FNCDs, we consider the preparation of an advanced security ink for information printing and anti-counterfeiting. Encoding/reading experiments were performed using a commercial inkjet printer (HP Deskjet 1112), and FNCDs and NCDs aqueous solution as ink and interference ink, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the number “8888” is designed to be 1895 (The founding time of Tianjin University) and the rest part. The number “8888” was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp excitation because of the same blue fluorescence background of the two inks. The printed security number “1895” was immediately visualized after turning off the UV light. Similarly, Fig. 4b shows the information security about letters. The letters “TJU FOCC LAB” stand for “The Laboratory of Functional Organic Carbon Composites, Tianjin university.” Under a 365 nm UV light, all letters were visible, but due to the inherent emission differences between fluorescence and RTP, and unique photoluminescence properties of different materials, “job” appears after the UV light is extinguished. Thus, with the inherent advantages of self-protection RTP, these CDs have broad prospects in advanced information security applications, requiring long-lived excited states.

Based on the long-lived excited state and self-protection RTP emission characteristics, FNCDs have attracted significant attention in advanced anti-counterfeiting monochrome printing. All kinds of complex patterns (e.g., QR codes, words, paintings, etc.) can be functionalized to have the phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting ability. As shown in Fig. 4c, the QR code was printed on A4 paper with high resolution by HP Deskjet 1112 printer, where the black ink cartridge was injected with aqueous FNCDs. The bright blue QR code was displayed under the UV lamp radiation, and the self-protective green RTP QR code appeared when the UV lamp was switched off. The QR code obtained the stored information “hello” through scanning using a recent smartphone indicating that information can be encrypted many times and transmitted without warning through this material. As shown in Fig. 4d, the outline of the school logo could not be recognized under the daily light, whereas that of the bright blue school logo was observed under the UV light. The RTP signal was visible to the naked eye for more than 5 s after the UV light was switched off. A paragraph and some pictures were also printed to prove that FNCDs ink has high resolution and is suitable for most occasions (Figure S26, supporting information). These results show that FNCDs can be used in the field of information storage and anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, we prepared an FNCDs-based material with RTP lifetime and quantum yield of 1.14 s and 8.3% through a combination of the solvothermal method and further gas-phase fluorination. Since N and F dopant atoms provide effective ISC efficiency and stable triplet excitons, the conversion of fluorescent materials to phosphorescent materials by gas-phase fluorination is realized. We also verified that the RTP properties of FNCDs are derived from the π→π∗ and n→π∗ electron transitions of C–N/CN, which are mainly attributed to the small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states. After that, the formation of semi-ionic C–F bonds enhanced intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonding and reduced the nonradiative energy loss. Also, the FNCDs aqueous solution exhibited fluorescence stability under different pH conditions, and the phosphorescence intensity of an FNCDs-coated paper can be maintained at 87.3% under 328 nm irradiation for 1 h, indicating that FNCDs materials have good photostability and can adapt to different environments. Because NCDs and FNCDs have the same bright blue fluorescence, although only phosphorescence was observed in FNCDs, we demonstrated that aqueous FNCDs can act as an advanced security ink for information printing and anti-counterfeiting. This research may not only provide a new method for the preparation of RTP carbon dots materials but also provide an approach for synthesizing other organic materials through halogen doping to improve ISC efficiency and stabilize triplet excitons.

Feng Liu: Formal analysis, Writing – original draftwrote the manuscript, structure of the materials. Zeyu Li: Formal analysis, structure of the materials. Yu Li: Formal analysis. Yiyu Feng: Writing – original draft. Wei Feng: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2016YFA0202302), the State Key Program of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51633007), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51773147 and 51973151).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.05.023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable-emission amorphous room-temperature phosphorescent polymers based on thermoreversible dynamic Covalent Bonds
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Lin, J. Wang, B. Ding, X. Ma, H. Tian, Tunable-emission amorphous room-temperature phosphorescent polymers based on thermoreversible dynamic Covalent Bonds, Angew. Chemie - Int. Ed. (2020) 3459-3463.
